description_long: This object consists of a cluster of thin, woody stems radiating outward from a central point, each stem adorned with numerous small, spherical buds or berries. The stems are light brown with a slightly reddish hue, and they arch gently, giving the structure a sprawling, spider-like appearance. The buds are pale green to grayish-white, evenly spaced along the length of each stem, and appear to be unopened or in an early flowering stage. There are no visible leaves, petals, or large floral structures, suggesting this may be a decorative or early-stage flowering branch, possibly resembling a sprig of pussy willow or a similar plant. The overall form is low and wide, with the stems spreading horizontally rather than vertically. This type of object could be found as part of urban landscaping, in planters, or as decorative ground cover in parks and public spaces.
description: A sprawling cluster of thin, woody stems radiating outward, each adorned with small, pale buds. The structure is low, wide, and resembles a decorative or early-stage flowering branch.
description_view_0: From this view, the radiating stems and evenly spaced buds are clearly visible, emphasizing the low, wide spread of the structure.
description_view_1: This angle highlights the arching nature of the stems and the density of buds along each branch.
description_view_2: The view shows the central convergence of the stems and the uniform distribution of buds, with a clear view of the branching pattern.
description_view_3: This perspective accentuates the radial symmetry and the outward splay of the stems, with buds visible along the entire length.
attribute_petal_arrangement: No visible petals; the structure consists of buds or unopened flowers arranged along the length of radiating stems in a whorled, radial pattern.
